title: Wafer-scale hybrid molecular beam epitaxy of BaTiO3 and SrTiO3 on silicon
authors: Xiaodong Tian, Yan Lin, Hanbin Gao, Han Yu, Yunpeng Ma, Ruiqi Liang, Changfu Chen, Wei Li, Chenguang Deng, Qiang Zheng, Qian Li
abstract: The integration of epitaxial barium titanate (BTO) on silicon represents a highly promising pathway for next-generation, energy-efficient photonic integrated circuits due to BTO's exceptionally high Pockels coefficients. However, the scalable epitaxy of BTO on Si remains hindered by complex stoichiometric control and slow growth rates. In this work, we demonstrate the continuous, uniform wafer-scale growth of high-quality BTO films on SrTiO3 (STO)-buffered 4-inch Si(001) wafers using a fully hybrid molecular beam epitaxy (hMBE) approach. By utilizing titanium tetraisopropoxide as a titanium precursor, we achieve a self-regulating, adsorption-controlled layer-by-layer growth at rates exceeding 75 nm/h, while maintaining an atomically sharp and structurally coherent BTO/STO interface. We systematically compare the structural, ferroelectric, and electro-optic (EO) properties of fully hMBE-grown BTO with those deposited via pulsed laser deposition (PLD) on identical STO/Si templates. While both techniques yield high-quality c-domain dominated films, the optimized hMBE-grown BTO exhibits superior crystallinity and a larger effective EO coefficient of 248 pm/V, surpassing that of the PLD-grown films (220 pm/V). These results highlight the advantages of the fully hMBE approach as a scalable, deterministic, and high-performance materials platform for wafer-scale integrated ferroelectric photonics.
summary: This paper presents a breakthrough in the scalable production of ferroelectric thin films for silicon photonics. By using a hybrid molecular beam epitaxy (hMBE) technique with a metalorganic titanium precursor, the researchers achieved high-quality, uniform BTO growth on 4-inch silicon wafers. The method provides much faster growth rates and better crystalline quality than traditional techniques like PLD. This advancement is crucial for developing next-generation, energy-efficient integrated photonic circuits.
main_problem: Achieving scalable, high-quality, and uniform epitaxial growth of barium titanate (BTO) on 4-inch silicon wafers for integrated photonics, while overcoming challenges in stoichiometry control and slow growth rates.
main_result: Demonstrated continuous, wafer-scale growth of high-quality BTO on SrTiO3-buffered Si using hybrid molecular beam epitaxy (hMBE), achieving superior crystallinity and a larger effective electro-optic coefficient (248 pm/V) compared to PLD-grown films.
method: A hybrid molecular beam epitaxy (hMBE) approach using titanium tetraisopropoxide (TTIP) as a metalorganic precursor, combined with Sr-passivation of Si(001) and atomic oxygen from an RF plasma source.
model_or_system: The experimental platform consists of BTO/SrTiO3/Si heterostructures grown on 4-inch Si(001) wafers, utilizing a self-regulating, adsorption-controlled growth regime.
key_observables: Effective electro-optic (EO) coefficient, growth rate, lattice parameters, surface roughness, domain configurations (a-domain vs. c-domain), and optical refractive indices.
important_parameters: Growth rate (>75 nm/h), TTIP/Ba flux ratio, temperature (600-800 C), and lattice mismatch/rotation (45 degrees).
main_assumptions: The growth is governed by a self-regulating, adsorption-controlled mechanism where the growth rate is limited by the Ba flux.
figures_summary: The figures likely illustrate the growth process, RHEED patterns for in-situ monitoring, XRD mapping for wafer-scale uniformity, STEM images of the BTO/STO/Si interface, AFM/PFM for surface morphology and domains, and electro-optic response measurements.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of BTO/Si integration, describes the hMBE experimental setup and precursor use, details the substrate preparation and growth process, compares hMBE with PLD/sputtering, analyzes structural and chemical properties, and concludes with the functional electro-optic performance.
